question.title: Consecutive Logins
question.desc: Find all users who have logged in consecutively for at least 3 days. The login table contains user_id and login_date. The output should include user_id and the count of consecutive logins.
question.solution: WITH ranked_logins AS ( SELECT user_id, login_date, DATE_PART('day', login_date - LAG(login_date) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY login_date)) AS diff FROM login ) SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) AS consecutive_logins FROM ranked_logins WHERE diff = 1 GROUP BY user_id HAVING COUNT(*) >= 2
question.tips: Consider using a window function to calculate the difference between consecutive login dates.
hints: Use a window function to calculate the difference between consecutive login dates.
You will need to partition the data by user_id to ensure that the window function operates on each user's logins separately.
After calculating the differences, you can use a HAVING clause to filter the results to only include users with at least 3 consecutive logins.

tests.setup: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS login; CREATE TABLE login (user_id INTEGER, login_date DATE); INSERT INTO login (user_id, login_date) VALUES (4, '2022-02-01'), (4, '2022-02-02'), (4, '2022-02-03'), (4, '2022-02-04'), (5, '2022-02-05'), (5, '2022-02-07');

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS login; CREATE TABLE login (user_id INTEGER, login_date DATE); INSERT INTO login (user_id, login_date) VALUES (6, '2022-03-01'), (6, '2022-03-02'), (7, '2022-03-03'), (7, '2022-03-04'), (7, '2022-03-05');
